chemicals.name: Because leeks belong to the Allium genus, they share the exact same toxic principles as onions, garlic

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: It is described as a "very poisonous" organosulfur compound and is considered a principal toxin in Allium species34. Cats are exceptionally vulnerable to this toxin because normal feline hemoglobin is about two to three times more susceptible to oxidative damage than the hemoglobin of other species
chemicals.description: It acts as a highly reactive oxidant that causes a marked decrease in the activity of the enzyme glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) in feline red blood cells34. This decrease drops the concentration of glutathione, which the cells need to prevent oxidative damage34. As a result, hydrogen peroxide oxidizes the hemoglobin, causing it to denature and form clumps known as Heinz bodies on the cel
chemicals.name: Brief description of its mechanism of action

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: This is also classified as a "very poisonous" compound34. Notably, it has been isolated specifically from boiled Allium materials, proving that these reactive toxins easily survive high temperatures and cooking processes
chemicals.description: Similar to N-propyl disulfide, it reduces glutathione concentrations in erythrocytes34. It significantly increases methemoglobin concentrations and Heinz body counts in the blood, leading to subsequent oxidative cell damage and hemolytic anemia1.... Any notes on concentration or potency: This is also classified as a "very poisonous" compound34. Notably, it has been isolated specifically from boile
chemicals.name: Sodium n-propylthiosulfate

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: It is another primary causative agent that has been implicated in Allium-induced hemolytic anemia
chemicals.description: Like other Allium organosulfur compounds, it is readily absorbed through the cat's gastrointestinal tract and metabolized into highly reactive oxidants that trigger oxidative hemolysis12. Any notes on concentration or potency: It is another primary causative agent that has been implicated in Allium-induced hemolytic anemia
chemicals.name: Brief description of its mechanism of action

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: These compounds are responsible for the characteristic flavor and odor of leeks67. Because they are present throughout the plant tissue and convert to toxins instantly upon damage, no part of the leek is safe for a cat.
chemicals.description: These are the non-toxic parent compounds sequestered inside the plant's cells67. When the leek experiences mechanical trauma—such as being chewed by a cat or chopped with a knife—these precursors instantly convert into the highly reactive, cell-damaging sulfur-containing organic compounds listed above67. Any notes on concentration or potency: These compounds are responsible for the characteristic
chemicals.name: Alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides (Precursors)

description: Leeks (Allium ampeloprasum) are a member of the Allium family — the same group that includes garlic, onions, and chives — all of which are toxic to cats. Leeks contain N-propyl disulfide and related organosulfur compounds that oxidise feline haemoglobin, causing Heinz body haemolytic anaemia. All forms are dangerous: raw, cooked, and dried leeks.
emergencyNote: Because leeks belong to the Allium genus, their toxicosis causes the exact same oxidative damage as onions and garlic. There is no specific antidote for Allium poisoning
name: Leeks
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: N-propyl disulfide and organosulfur compounds (alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides); all parts toxic including the bulb, stems, and leaves
Mechanism: oxidative damage to haemoglobin creates Heinz bodies; the spleen destroys affected red blood cells faster than they can be replaced, causing haemolytic anaemia
Symptoms: lethargy, weakness, pale or yellowish gums, rapid breathing, loss of appetite, reddish-brown urine; onset often delayed 3–5 days after ingestion
Leeks in soups, stews, or stir-fries remain toxic after cooking
Never share leek-containing foods with cats; contact your vet immediately if ingestion is suspected

symptoms.name: Because leeks belong to the Allium genus, their toxicosis presents with the exact same clinical signs as onions, garlic
Vomiting, Diarrhea, Abdominal Pain, and Loss of Appetite
symptoms.notes: These are typically the first clinical signs of toxicosis to appear12. They occur due to direct irritation of the gastrointestinal tract and frequently result in dehydration
symptoms.onset: Usually within 1 day (24 hours) of ingestion.

symptoms.name: Lethargy, Weakness, and Depression
symptoms.notes: These symptoms emerge as a secondary response to systemic hypoxia caused by the profound loss of oxygen-carrying red blood cells
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days.

symptoms.name: Pale Mucous Membranes and Jaundice (Icterus)
symptoms.notes: Pale gums indicate a lack of oxygenated blood (anemia)23. Jaundice (yellowing of the skin or mucosa) occurs because of severe red blood cell destruction (extravascular hemolysis), which leads to an excess of bilirubin in the blood (hyperbilirubinemia)
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days.

symptoms.name: Tachypnea and Dyspnea (Breathing Difficulties)
symptoms.notes: This is a compensatory response as the cat's body attempts to offset the decreased blood oxygen transportation capacity and resulting tissue hypoxia
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days.

symptoms.name: Tachycardia (Rapid Heart Rate)
symptoms.notes: Tachycardia develops as the heart pumps much faster to compensate for the impaired oxygen transportation caused by the ruptured and damaged red blood cells
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days.

symptoms.name: Hemoglobinuria (Dark Urine)
symptoms.notes: This is a hallmark sign of severe oxidative intravascular hemolysis34. Damaged red blood cells rupture and release hemoglobin, which is then excreted through the urinary tract47. This process can lead to secondary acute kidney damage
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days.

symptoms.name: Cold Sensitivity
symptoms.notes: Affected cats may have difficulty regulating their body temperature as a systemic effect of the progressive anemia and impaired oxygen delivery to tissues

symptoms.name: Allium Breath Odor
symptoms.notes: While not harmful itself, this symptom is specifically associated with recent ingestion and serves as a highly valuable diagnostic clue for veterinarians evaluating the cat

treatments.name: Because leeks belong to the Allium genus, their toxicosis causes the exact same oxidative damage as onions and garlic. There is no specific antidote for Allium poisoning

treatments.description: of the procedure: Providing the cat with supplemental oxygen and transfusing whole blood to restore the body's depleted oxygen-carrying capacity23. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Oxygen Therapy and Blood Transfusions (Life-Saving Stabilization)
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent priority for severely affected cats presenting with life-threatening clinical signs of profound hemolytic anemia, methemoglobinemia, or respiratory distress

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering an emetic drug under veterinary supervision to force the cat to expel the ingested leeks before the toxins are fully absorbed. For cats, the recommended drug is dexmedetomidine (administered intramuscularly or intravenously)4. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This early decontamination step is highly valuable but is only indicated if the cat is currently asymptomatic and the ingestion was very recent (caught within one to two hours)24. Veterinarians should prioritize drugs specifically licensed for cats to avoid unintended adverse events

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering oral activated charcoal (typically 1 to 4 g/kg) to bind to any residual toxins remaining in the stomach and gastrointestinal tract2. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This is typically administered directly after inducing emesis to trap toxins before they can enter the bloodstream2. However, it should be noted that its actual efficacy in adsorbing or preventing the production of Allium-derived sulfides is currently unknown

treatments.description: of the procedure: Hospitalizing the cat to administer continuous IV fluids to rehydrate the animal, support blood pressure, and maintain proper renal perfusion23. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy (Systemic Supportive Care)
treatments.notes: This treatment is indicated if the cat presents with hypotension or has suffered extensive fluid loss from severe vomiting and diarrhea2. Furthermore, it is a critical protective measure if the cat develops hemoglobinuria (dark urine caused by ruptured red blood cells), as IV diuresis helps protect

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering anti-sickness/anti-nausea medications alongside antioxidant vitamins (such as Vitamin C and E) or N-acetyl-cysteine26. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Antioxidant and Gastrointestinal Medications (Symptomatic Relief)
treatments.notes: Anti-emetics are utilized if the cat is suffering from persistent vomiting2. While antioxidants are sometimes administered to combat the oxidative damage done to the red blood cells, they have shown minimal proven protective effects against Allium toxicosis in cats
